summary.text: The dominant pattern today is a sudden breakout in the AI Agent Framework category, led by 'ai-engineering-from-scratch', which gained 6,898 stars in a single day—a 3x+ acceleration anomaly. This project offers a massive free curriculum (503 lessons, 20 phases) from math foundations to autonomous swarms, with every lesson producing a reusable artifact. The attention suggests developers are hungry for comprehensive, structured learning paths rather than fragmented tool tutorials. Meanwhile, 'headroom' (coding agent category) continues its sustained hot streak with +4,050 stars, confirming ongoing interest in token optimization for LLM workflows. The absence of any Product Hunt or Hacker News cross-source signals today makes the GitHub breakout even more notable—it's purely organic developer attention. If this curriculum maintains momentum, it may indicate a broader shift toward systematic AI engineering education.

projects.name: ai-engineering-from-scratch
projects.description: Learn it. Build it. Ship it for others.

llmSummary.oneLiner: A massive, free, open-source curriculum (503 lessons, 20 phases) teaching AI engineering from math foundations to autonomous swarms, with every lesson producing a reusable artifact.
llmSummary.problemSolved: Bridges the gap between scattered AI tutorials and a cohesive, deep understanding—students learn by building algorithms from scratch before using frameworks, ending each lesson with a practical tool (prompt, skill, agent, MCP server).
llmSummary.whyNow: The repo exploded from ~21k to 27.8k stars in a single day (24h delta: 6,708 stars, 7d avg delta: 1,942). This is a sudden breakout with low acceleration (1.0), indicating a massive attention spike likely from a single external event (e.g., HN front page, viral tweet, or major influencer mention). The project is actively maintained (commits within 7 days) and has a polished README with a clear value proposition.
llmSummary.whatChanged: No recent release or obvious commit trigger. The sudden star surge (6,708 in 24h) suggests external amplification—likely a high-traffic link from Hacker News, Reddit, or a prominent AI educator. The README was already comprehensive, so the trigger is external visibility, not a repo change.
llmSummary.whyTrending: Offers a rare combination: depth (from-scratch implementations), breadth (20 phases covering math, ML, vision, NLP, agents, swarms, production), and practicality (503 reusable artifacts). It directly addresses the common complaint that AI education is too shallow or too framework-dependent.
llmSummary.developerTakeaway: This is a goldmine for any developer wanting to truly understand AI internals. The structured curriculum and 'build it, use it, ship it' loop make it ideal for self-study or team upskilling. The agent skills (e.g., /find-your-level) lower the barrier to entry.
llmSummary.developerInsight: Signals a growing demand for 'deep stack' AI education—developers want to move beyond API wrappers and understand the math and algorithms. Projects that combine rigor with practical output (artifacts, not just notes) are likely to gain traction.
llmSummary.targetUsers: Software engineers, data scientists, and students who want to build AI systems from scratch and understand the underlying theory, not just use APIs.
llmSummary.riskNotes: Massive scope (503 lessons) may lead to incomplete or inconsistent quality across phases. The sudden star spike could attract unrealistic expectations. Long-term maintenance of such a large curriculum is a challenge.

projects.name: headroom
projects.description: Compress tool outputs, logs, files, and RAG chunks before they reach the LLM. 60-95% fewer tokens, same answers. Library, proxy, MCP server.

llmSummary.oneLiner: Headroom compresses tool outputs, logs, files, and RAG chunks before they reach the LLM, reducing tokens by 60-95% while preserving answer quality.
llmSummary.problemSolved: LLM context windows fill up quickly with verbose tool outputs, logs, and RAG chunks, leading to high costs, latency, and context truncation. Headroom solves this by compressing content locally before sending it to the LLM, with reversible compression (CCR) so the LLM can retrieve originals on demand.
llmSummary.whyNow: Headroom gained 3,316 stars in the last 24 hours, reaching 9,642 total stars with 635 forks. The project has a sustained hot trend with low acceleration (1.0), indicating steady organic growth rather than a sudden spike. Recent release and commits within 7 days show active maintenance. The high star velocity suggests strong community adoption among AI agent users.
llmSummary.whatChanged: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. The recent release and continuous commits indicate ongoing improvements, but the star acceleration is low, suggesting steady attention rather than a viral event.
llmSummary.whyTrending: Headroom addresses a universal pain point for AI agent users: token costs and context limits. It offers multiple integration modes (library, proxy, MCP server, agent wrap) and supports all major coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Aider, Copilot). The 60-95% token reduction with preserved accuracy on benchmarks like GSM8K and SQuAD v2 makes it a practical tool for daily use.
llmSummary.developerTakeaway: Headroom is a drop-in solution to reduce LLM costs and latency without changing agent code. Developers can wrap their existing agent or use the proxy mode for zero-code integration. The reversible compression (CCR) ensures no data loss, and cross-agent memory enables shared context across multiple agents.
llmSummary.developerInsight: This signals a shift toward local preprocessing layers for LLM interactions. As agents become more autonomous and handle larger contexts, compression middleware like Headroom will become essential infrastructure. The multi-agent memory feature points to a future where agents share compressed context stores, reducing redundant processing.
llmSummary.targetUsers: AI developers using coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Aider, Copilot), teams building RAG pipelines, and anyone integrating LLMs into applications who wants to reduce token consumption and costs.
llmSummary.riskNotes: The project is relatively new (9.6k stars) and may have undiscovered edge cases. Compression algorithms may not preserve all nuances in complex code or domain-specific content. The local proxy and MCP server add operational overhead. Dependency on Python 3.10+ and optional ML model (Kompress-base) may limit some environments.

projects.name: Open-LLM-VTuber
projects.description: Talk to any LLM with hands-free voice interaction, voice interruption, and Live2D taking face running locally across platforms

llmSummary.oneLiner: Open-LLM-VTuber is a voice-interactive AI companion with a Live2D avatar that runs locally across platforms, supporting offline LLM, ASR, and TTS.
llmSummary.problemSolved: It provides a fully offline, customizable AI companion with real-time voice interaction, visual perception, and a Live2D avatar, eliminating the need for cloud services and enabling privacy-preserving, cross-platform use.
llmSummary.whyNow: The project has 8,834 stars and gained 728 stars in the last 24 hours, indicating strong and sustained attention. The acceleration ratio is 1 (low), suggesting steady organic growth rather than a sudden spike. This consistent velocity makes it a noteworthy project for anyone interested in local AI companions.
llmSummary.whatChanged: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. The README mentions active v2.0 development planning, which may be drawing continued interest, but no recent release or external buzz is detected.
llmSummary.whyTrending: It combines multiple hot trends: local LLMs, voice interaction, Live2D avatars, and AI companionship, all in one open-source package that runs offline. The cross-platform support and extensive model integration make it accessible to a wide audience.
llmSummary.developerTakeaway: This project demonstrates the growing demand for personalized, offline AI companions. Developers can learn from its modular architecture (LLM, ASR, TTS) and consider building similar customizable, privacy-focused AI interfaces.
llmSummary.developerInsight: The success of Open-LLM-VTuber signals a shift toward local-first, multimodal AI applications that prioritize user privacy and customization. Developers should explore integrating Live2D or similar avatars with LLMs to create engaging, interactive experiences.
llmSummary.targetUsers: AI enthusiasts, VTuber fans, developers building AI companions, privacy-conscious users, and anyone wanting a customizable, offline voice-interactive AI.
llmSummary.riskNotes: The project is in early stages (v1) with a major v2.0 rewrite underway, which may cause instability or breaking changes. The README warns about HTTPS requirements for remote access and potential licensing issues with Live2D sample models for commercial use.

llmSummary.whatChanged: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. Recent commits within 7 days and ongoing community activity suggest continuous improvement, but no major release or external event is evident from the metadata.
llmSummary.whyTrending: Odoo's massive star count and daily star velocity reflect its status as a leading open-source ERP. It consistently attracts attention from businesses seeking affordable, customizable business management solutions.
llmSummary.developerTakeaway: Odoo's modular architecture and Python-based framework make it a strong candidate for building custom business apps or extending ERP functionality. Developers can leverage its ORM, web services, and extensive module ecosystem.
llmSummary.developerInsight: For AI developers, Odoo's integration points (e.g., APIs, module system) offer opportunities to embed AI features like predictive analytics, chatbots, or automation into business workflows, tapping into a large existing user base.
llmSummary.targetUsers: Businesses of all sizes, ERP consultants, Python developers building business applications, and organizations seeking an open-source alternative to proprietary ERP systems.

projects.name: ncnn
projects.description: ncnn is a high-performance neural network inference framework optimized for the mobile platform

llmSummary.oneLiner: ncnn is a high-performance neural network inference framework optimized for mobile, embedded, and desktop deployment, with no third-party runtime dependencies and support for CPU and Vulkan GPU backends.
llmSummary.problemSolved: ncnn solves the problem of deploying deep learning models efficiently on resource-constrained devices (phones, embedded systems, edge devices) without heavy dependencies, enabling low-latency inference across Android, iOS, Linux, Windows, and more.
llmSummary.whyNow: ncnn is experiencing a sudden breakout with an acceleration ratio of 6.6 (high) and a 24-hour star delta of 11, indicating a sharp increase in attention. With 23,329 total stars and a recent release within 30 days, the project is gaining momentum likely due to its expanding platform support (HarmonyOS, visionOS, watchOS) and the growing need for on-device AI inference.
llmSummary.whatChanged: The project has a recent release (within 30 days) and active commits (within 7 days). The README shows expanded build support for HarmonyOS, visionOS, watchOS, and tvOS, which may have attracted new users. No single external trigger is identifiable, but the acceleration pattern suggests a surge in interest from the mobile/edge AI community.
llmSummary.whyTrending: ncnn is trending because it provides a lightweight, cross-platform inference solution that is production-proven in Tencent apps (QQ, WeChat). The recent addition of support for Apple's visionOS and HarmonyOS, combined with the ease of converting PyTorch/ONNX models via pnnx, makes it a go-to choice for developers deploying AI on diverse devices.
llmSummary.developerTakeaway: ncnn is a mature, battle-tested framework for on-device inference. Developers should consider it for mobile or edge AI projects where low latency and minimal dependencies are critical. The pnnx tool simplifies model conversion from PyTorch/ONNX, lowering the barrier to entry.
llmSummary.developerInsight: This signals a shift toward multi-platform AI deployment, where frameworks like ncnn enable a single model to run across phones, wearables, and embedded systems. Developers should watch for further expansion into new hardware backends (RISC-V, Vulkan) and tighter integration with popular training frameworks.
llmSummary.targetUsers: Mobile app developers, embedded systems engineers, AI researchers deploying models on edge devices, and developers working on cross-platform inference for Android, iOS, Linux, Windows, macOS, HarmonyOS, and Apple's visionOS.
llmSummary.riskNotes: The project has 1,177 open issues, which may indicate maintenance challenges or feature requests. The C++ API may have a steeper learning curve for Python-first developers, though Python bindings are available. Vulkan GPU support may not be available on all devices.

projects.name: ColossalAI
projects.description: Making large AI models cheaper, faster and more accessible

llmSummary.oneLiner: Colossal-AI is an open-source deep learning system that makes large AI models cheaper, faster, and more accessible through advanced parallelism and heterogeneous memory management.
llmSummary.problemSolved: Training and deploying large AI models (e.g., LLMs, video generation models) is prohibitively expensive and complex. Colossal-AI reduces cost and time by providing efficient distributed training and inference with minimal code changes.
llmSummary.whyNow: The repo has a sudden breakout with an acceleration of 6 (high), despite a low 7-day average star delta of -0.6. With 41,385 total stars and 4,505 forks, it's a well-established project that recently saw a spike in attention, possibly due to new benchmarks (B200 GPUs showing 50-70% throughput gains) or the release of Open-Sora and DeepSeek fine-tuning guides.
llmSummary.whatChanged: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. However, recent README updates highlight new benchmarks on B200 GPUs, DeepSeek 671B fine-tuning, and Open-Sora video generation, which may have driven the breakout.
llmSummary.whyTrending: It offers a practical, cost-saving solution for training large models (e.g., 30% cost reduction with FP8), supports cutting-edge hardware (B200, H200), and provides turnkey examples for popular models like LLaMA, GPT, and Stable Diffusion.
llmSummary.developerTakeaway: Colossal-AI is a powerful tool for developers who need to scale model training or inference without deep distributed systems expertise. Its support for multiple parallelism strategies and heterogeneous memory makes it versatile for both research and production.
llmSummary.developerInsight: This signals a shift toward democratizing large model training: developers can now achieve near-linear scaling on multi-GPU setups with minimal code changes, reducing the barrier to entry for building foundation models.
llmSummary.targetUsers: AI researchers, ML engineers, and startups working on large-scale model training or inference, especially those using PyTorch and seeking cost-effective GPU utilization.
llmSummary.riskNotes: The project is backed by a company (HPC-AI Tech) that also offers paid cloud services, which may lead to feature prioritization for commercial users. Additionally, the rapid pace of change in AI infrastructure could make some parallelism strategies obsolete.

projects.name: claude-task-master
projects.description: An AI-powered task-management system you can drop into Cursor, Lovable, Windsurf, Roo, and others.

llmSummary.oneLiner: An AI-powered task-management system that integrates directly into Cursor, Lovable, Windsurf, and other AI coding environments via MCP.
llmSummary.problemSolved: Developers using AI coding assistants lack a structured way to manage, prioritize, and track tasks across sessions. Taskmaster provides a persistent task hierarchy, dependency management, and research capabilities that work inside the editor's AI chat.
llmSummary.whyNow: With 27,315 stars and a 7-day average delta of 7.1 stars/day, the project shows sustained high attention. The acceleration ratio of 2.8 indicates a sudden breakout — interest is intensifying rapidly. The 24-hour delta of 11 stars confirms continued momentum.
llmSummary.whatChanged: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. The README was recently updated (pushed 2026-04-28) and the project has a steady stream of commits, but no recent release or external buzz event is detectable.
llmSummary.whyTrending: It solves a real pain point for the growing number of developers using AI coding assistants: how to keep tasks organized and reproducible. The one-click MCP install for Cursor 1.0+ lowers friction dramatically, and the support for multiple AI providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc.) makes it broadly compatible.
llmSummary.developerTakeaway: If you use Cursor, Windsurf, or any MCP-compatible editor, this is a drop-in tool to bring structured task management into your AI workflow. It's worth trying for any project that involves complex, multi-step AI-assisted development.
llmSummary.developerInsight: The rise of MCP-based tools like Taskmaster signals a shift from ad-hoc AI chat to structured, persistent workflows. Developers who adopt these patterns early may gain a significant productivity edge.
llmSummary.targetUsers: Developers using AI coding assistants (Cursor, Windsurf, Lovable, Roo, Claude Code) who want to manage tasks, dependencies, and research within their editor.
llmSummary.riskNotes: The project depends on external AI API keys (Anthropic, OpenAI, etc.) and the MCP ecosystem, which is still evolving. There are 194 open issues, suggesting active development but also some rough edges. The license is MIT with Commons Clause, which may restrict commercial use.

projects.name: DeepSeek-Reasonix
projects.description: DeepSeek-native AI coding agent for your terminal. Engineered around prefix-cache stability — leave it running.

llmSummary.oneLiner: A DeepSeek-native AI coding agent for the terminal, rewritten in Go for performance and prefix-cache stability.
llmSummary.problemSolved: Provides a configurable, plugin-driven AI coding assistant that leverages DeepSeek's prefix caching to reduce token costs during long sessions, with a single static binary for easy distribution.
llmSummary.whyNow: With 17,569 stars, a 24-hour delta of +904, and a 7-day average delta of 1,295.8, this project is experiencing sustained high growth. The acceleration ratio of 1 indicates steady, organic interest rather than a sudden spike. It ranks in the top 2-3 in velocity across Agents, LLMs, and CLI categories on OOSMetrics, signaling strong community adoption.
llmSummary.whatChanged: The project underwent a ground-up rewrite from TypeScript to Go (version 1.0), which is now the default branch. This rewrite likely improved performance and stability, attracting attention. The README also highlights a migration guide and new features like MCP plugin support and sandboxing.
llmSummary.whyTrending: It combines the popularity of DeepSeek models with a well-engineered, config-driven terminal agent that emphasizes cost efficiency through prefix caching. The Go rewrite offers a single binary, easy install via npm or Homebrew, and extensive plugin support, making it practical for developers.
llmSummary.developerTakeaway: A practical, production-ready AI coding agent optimized for DeepSeek, with a focus on low token costs and extensibility via plugins and MCP.
llmSummary.developerInsight: This signals a shift toward specialized, model-native agents that optimize for cost and performance. Developers should consider prefix caching and config-driven architectures to build efficient AI tools.
llmSummary.targetUsers: Developers using DeepSeek models who want a terminal-based AI coding assistant with plugin support and cost-efficient long sessions.
llmSummary.riskNotes: Heavy reliance on DeepSeek's API and prefix caching; changes to DeepSeek's pricing or API could impact value. The project is still early (v1.0 rewrite) with 564 open issues, indicating active development but potential instability.

llmSummary.developerTakeaway: ECC provides a production-ready, open-source framework to standardize and enhance AI agent workflows, reducing context-switching overhead and improving agent reliability across different tools.
llmSummary.developerInsight: This signals a shift toward unified agent orchestration layers that abstract away harness-specific quirks, enabling developers to build once and run anywhere. hotProjects.name: headroom
hotProjects.description: Compress tool outputs, logs, files, and RAG chunks before they reach the LLM. 60-95% fewer tokens, same answers. Library, proxy, MCP server.

llmSummary.oneLiner: Headroom compresses tool outputs, logs, files, and RAG chunks before they reach the LLM, reducing tokens by 60-95% while preserving answer quality.
llmSummary.problemSolved: LLM context windows fill up quickly with verbose tool outputs, logs, and RAG chunks, leading to high costs, latency, and context truncation. Headroom solves this by compressing content locally before sending it to the LLM, with reversible compression (CCR) so the LLM can retrieve originals on demand.
llmSummary.whyNow: Headroom gained 3,316 stars in the last 24 hours, reaching 9,642 total stars with 635 forks. The project has a sustained hot trend with low acceleration (1.0), indicating steady organic growth rather than a sudden spike. Recent release and commits within 7 days show active maintenance. The high star velocity suggests strong community adoption among AI agent users.
llmSummary.whatChanged: No single obvious trigger — the project appears to be sustaining organic growth. The recent release and continuous commits indicate ongoing improvements, but the star acceleration is low, suggesting steady attention rather than a viral event.
llmSummary.whyTrending: Headroom addresses a universal pain point for AI agent users: token costs and context limits. It offers multiple integration modes (library, proxy, MCP server, agent wrap) and supports all major coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Aider, Copilot). The 60-95% token reduction with preserved accuracy on benchmarks like GSM8K and SQuAD v2 makes it a practical tool for daily use.
llmSummary.developerTakeaway: Headroom is a drop-in solution to reduce LLM costs and latency without changing agent code. Developers can wrap their existing agent or use the proxy mode for zero-code integration. The reversible compression (CCR) ensures no data loss, and cross-agent memory enables shared context across multiple agents.
llmSummary.developerInsight: This signals a shift toward local preprocessing layers for LLM interactions. As agents become more autonomous and handle larger contexts, compression middleware like Headroom will become essential infrastructure. The multi-agent memory feature points to a future where agents share compressed context stores, reducing redundant processing.
llmSummary.targetUsers: AI developers using coding agents (Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, Aider, Copilot), teams building RAG pipelines, and anyone integrating LLMs into applications who wants to reduce token consumption and costs.
llmSummary.riskNotes: The project is relatively new (9.6k stars) and may have undiscovered edge cases. Compression algorithms may not preserve all nuances in complex code or domain-specific content. The local proxy and MCP server add operational overhead. Dependency on Python 3.10+ and optional ML model (Kompress-base) may limit some environments.

modelDemoSignals.title: STRIDE: Training Data Attribution via Sparse Recovery from Subset Perturbations
modelDemoSignals.description: Training Data Attribution (TDA) seeks to trace a model's predictions back to its training data. The gold standard for TDA relies on causal interventions, observing how a model changes when data is added or removed, but repeated retraining is computationally challenging for Large Language Models (LLMs). Consequently, most approaches approximate this effect in the parameter space using gradients. However, tracking gradients across billions of parameters is not only prohibitively expensive but reli
modelDemoSignals.summary: Training Data Attribution (TDA) seeks to trace a model's predictions back to its training data. The gold standard for TDA relies on causal interventions, observing how a model changes when data is added or removed, but repeated retraining is computationally challenging for Large Language Models (LLMs). Consequently, most approaches approximate this effect in the parameter space using gradients. However, tracking gradients across billions of parameters is not only prohibitively expensive but relies on local approximations. In this work, we propose a shift: rather than estimating parameter changes, we model the functional effect of training data in the activation space. We introduce STRIDE (Steering-based Training Data Influence Decomposition), a framework that formulates TDA as a sparse recovery problem in the spirit of compressive sensing. STRIDE learns lightweight "steering operators" that mimic the behavioral shift caused by training on data subsets. By measuring how these operators perturb test predictions, we recover individual training example influences via sparse linear decomposition. STRIDE achieves state-of-the-art for LLM pre-training attribution while being an order of magnitude ($13\times$) faster than previous art. We further validate its practical utility through downstream applications including data selection, data contamination, and qualitative analysis.
modelDemoSignals.author: Rishit Dagli, Abir Harrasse, Luke Zhang, Florent Draye, Amirali Abdullah, Bernhard Schölkopf, Zhijing Jin
modelDemoSignals.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: large language model, llm

modelDemoSignals.description: Multi-agent reasoning systems adopt a "generate-then-transfer" paradigm that forces end-to-end latency to scale linearly with pipeline depth. We introduce StreamMA, a multi-agent reasoning system that streams each reasoning step to downstream agents as soon as it is generated, pipelining adjacent agents and thus reducing latency. modelDemoSignals.summary: Multi-agent reasoning systems adopt a "generate-then-transfer" paradigm that forces end-to-end latency to scale linearly with pipeline depth. We introduce StreamMA, a multi-agent reasoning system that streams each reasoning step to downstream agents as soon as it is generated, pipelining adjacent agents and thus reducing latency. Surprisingly, this pipelining also improves effectiveness: because multi-step reasoning quality is non-uniform and early steps are more reliable than later ones, working with these reliable early steps instead of the full chain prevents error-prone late steps from misleading downstream agents. We formalize both advantages with the first closed-form joint analysis of stream, serial, and single protocols, deriving the effectiveness ordering, speedup upper bound, and cost ratio. Across eight reasoning benchmarks spanning mathematics, science, and code, two frontier LLMs (Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4), and three topologies (Chain, Tree, Graph), StreamMA outperforms both baselines (avg. +7.3 pp, max +22.4 pp on HMMT 2026; Claude Opus 4.6-high). Beyond these contributions, we discover a "step-level scaling law": increasing per-agent steps consistently improves both effectiveness and efficiency, a new scaling dimension orthogonal to and composable with agent-count scaling.
modelDemoSignals.author: Zhen Yang, Xiaogang Xu, Wen Wang, Cong Chen, Xander Xu, Ying-Cong Chen
modelDemoSignals.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: llm, agent, multi-agent, benchmark

modelDemoSignals.title: Reinforcement Learning from Rich Feedback with Distributional DAgger
modelDemoSignals.description: Reasoning models have advanced rapidly, but the dominant reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) recipe remains surprisingly narrow: sample many responses and reward each with a single bit indicating whether the final answer is correct. Yet many settings provide rich feedback, including execution traces, tool outputs, expert corrections, and model self-evaluations. We study how to use such feedback through a distributional variant of the classic imitation learning algorithm DAgger,
modelDemoSignals.summary: Reasoning models have advanced rapidly, but the dominant reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) recipe remains surprisingly narrow: sample many responses and reward each with a single bit indicating whether the final answer is correct. Yet many settings provide rich feedback, including execution traces, tool outputs, expert corrections, and model self-evaluations. We study how to use such feedback through a distributional variant of the classic imitation learning algorithm DAgger, where the learner has local access to an expert distribution on states visited by the current policy. This yields a simple forward cross-entropy objective that admits a blackbox expert and whose sequence-level gradient {conduct rich credit assignment by propagating} future expert-student disagreement back to earlier decisions. We show that prior RL with self-distillation objectives based on reverse KL or Jensen-Shannon fail to guarantee monotonic policy improvement: even when the expert has higher reward, their updates may increase probability on worse actions. In contrast, we show that forward cross-entropy admits monotonic policy improvement and enjoys guarantees on regret. We further show that our objective optimizes a lower bound on teacher-weighted likelihood of success, leading to improved Pass@N. Empirically, our approach, DistIL, improves over RLVR and RL with self-distillation baselines across a variety of domains: scientific reasoning, coding, and solving hard mathematical problems.
modelDemoSignals.author: Rishabh Agrawal, Jacob Fein-Ashley, Paria Rashidinejad

modelDemoSignals.title: Multi-Column RBF Neural Network Using Adaptive and Non-Adaptive Particle Swarm Optimization
modelDemoSignals.description: The radial basis function neural network (RBFN) trained with a gradient descending algorithm provides an effective fully connected structure in both shallow and deep networks. The error correction (ErrCor), a state-of-the-art gradient-based training method, selects optimal hidden units to improve accuracy. modelDemoSignals.summary: The radial basis function neural network (RBFN) trained with a gradient descending algorithm provides an effective fully connected structure in both shallow and deep networks. The error correction (ErrCor), a state-of-the-art gradient-based training method, selects optimal hidden units to improve accuracy. Alternatively, as a population-based algorithm, the particle swarm optimization algorithm (PSO) uses the swarm experience to optimize RBFN parameters, offering global search and robustness to local minima. Adaptive PSO (APSO) has emerged as an improved variant of PSO. APSO algorithm improves convergence speed by dynamically adjusting swarm parameters during optimization. Both ErrCor and PSO demonstrate improved results and competitive convergence. However, with large datasets, these methods face scalability challenges such as excessive kernel computations and large hidden layer structures. A recent multi-column RBFN approach (MCRN) improves ErrCor performance by deploying small RBFNs in a parallel system. Inspired by MCRN's success, we propose two novel approaches to improve PSO performance: the multi-column RBFN with PSO (MC-PSO) and the multi-column RBFN with APSO (MC-APSO). These methods introduce parallel RBFN structures trained using evolutionary swarm methods. Each RBFN is independently trained on a specific spatial subset of the dataset using either PSO or APSO algorithms. These resulting specialist-trained RBFNs are tailored to their respective subsets. During testing, only selected RBFNs, where the test instance neighbors are located, contribute to the multi-column output. This specialization improves accuracy, while parallelism enhances speed. We evaluate the proposed methods on various benchmark datasets. The MC-PSO and MC-APSO outperform ErrCor, PSO, APSO, and MCRN in terms of accuracy and recall. They also demonstrate faster training and testing times in most experiments.
modelDemoSignals.author: Ammar Hoori, Yuichi Motai

modelDemoSignals.title: An Open-Source Two-Stage Computer Vision Pipeline for Fine-Grained Vehicle Classification using Vision Transformers
modelDemoSignals.description: Vehicle body type is a significant determinant of cyclist injury severity in overtaking crashes, yet automated tools for classifying vehicles into injury-risk-relevant categories from naturalistic roadway video do not exist in the open literature. Standard object detection benchmarks provide only coarse vehicle labels (car, truck, bus, motorcycle), while existing fine-grained recognition systems are trained on controlled imagery and lack evaluation for deployment robustness across recording site
modelDemoSignals.summary: Vehicle body type is a significant determinant of cyclist injury severity in overtaking crashes, yet automated tools for classifying vehicles into injury-risk-relevant categories from naturalistic roadway video do not exist in the open literature. Standard object detection benchmarks provide only coarse vehicle labels (car, truck, bus, motorcycle), while existing fine-grained recognition systems are trained on controlled imagery and lack evaluation for deployment robustness across recording sites. This paper presents an open-source two-stage computer vision pipeline combining a pre-trained RT-DETR detector for coarse vehicle localization with a fine-tuned Vision Transformer (ViT-Base/16) for six-category body-type classification: passenger car, SUV, pickup truck, minivan, large van, and commercial truck. A confidence-based abstention mechanism withholds Stage 2 predictions when softmax output falls below 0.60, producing unknown labels rather than silent misclassifications. Evaluated on 3,805 annotated overtaking events from a bicycle-lane corridor in Ann Arbor, Michigan (in-distribution), the pipeline achieved 0.94 accuracy with per-class F1 scores from 0.91 (minivan) to 0.97 (SUV). On an independent out-of-distribution evaluation of 311 events from an open cycling dataset without retraining, accuracy was 0.89. Three of four well-represented categories maintained F1 at or above 0.90 under domain shift. The largest degradation was observed for minivan (F1 = 0.72), driven by abstention rate rising from 2.4% to 25.0% rather than active misclassification, consistent with the mechanism propagating genuine model uncertainty. The full pipeline, including inference scripts, training code, evaluation utilities, and model weights, is released as open-source software to support reproducibility and reuse across roadside video archives and cycling safety research.
modelDemoSignals.author: Gandhimathi Padmanaban, Fred Feng
modelDemoSignals.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: safety, benchmark, evaluation

aihotHighlights.title: Cloudflare Radar：机器人流量首次超过人类占比57.5%
aihotHighlights.summary: Cloudflare Radar 实时统计显示，过去一周（5月28日至6月4日）全球所有 HTML 网页请求流量中，57.5% 来自机器人（爬虫、AI 抓取、自动化脚本），仅42.5%来自真人浏览器，机器人流量首次超过人类。按所有 HTTP 流量返回内容分类，JSON（API 机器通信）占33.1%居首，HTML 仅12%。互联网流量主体已从人类浏览网页转向机器间通信和机器人抓取。

aihotHighlights.title: 联合国报告：2030年AI数据中心水电消耗将翻倍
aihotHighlights.summary: 联合国大学水、环境与健康研究所报告指出，受AI需求驱动，去年全球数据中心耗电448太瓦时（AI占五分之一），耗水4.5万亿升，碳排放1.89亿吨。预计到2030年，年耗电量将翻倍至945太瓦时（AI占40%），耗水增至9.3万亿升，碳排放升至3.99亿吨，占地面积从6900平方公里扩展至14500平方公里。报告警告若忽视环境成本，AI落地还将加剧土地紧张与电子废弃物问题。

aihotHighlights.title: 世界模型的功能分类
aihotHighlights.summary: World Labs团队与李飞飞发文，梳理"世界模型"这一被滥用的术语。对比语言模型学习文本统计，世界模型学习空间与时间统计（如光照、物理规律）。基于部分可观马尔可夫决策过程（POMDP）框架，智能体通过动作影响世界状态，观测是部分视图。当前被称为"世界模型"的不同系统本质上是同一循环的不同投影：第一类为渲染器，输出给人眼看的像素，以视觉保真度为核心。文章着重于概念分层，未给出具体模型名、参数或基准分数。

aihotHighlights.title: 洪水韧性新篇章：Google 开源水文建模框架
aihotHighlights.summary: Google Research 在 GitHub 开源了其水文建模框架，这是一个基于 PyTorch 的 Python 包，采用与 Flood Hub 河段洪水预报相同的模型架构和训练数据。该框架允许研究者和预报员训练 AI 洪水预报模型、添加新模型与数据，并已与捷克水文气象研究所（CHMI）等合作测试。开放模型架构和训练管线旨在让各国气象水文部门在保留数据完全控制权的同时，整合基于 AI 的洪水预报。

aihotHighlights.title: Anthropic 用 Claude 赋能自助数据分析
aihotHighlights.summary: Anthropic 使用 Claude 自动化了 95% 的业务分析查询，整体准确率约 95%。其关键在于构建智能体分析栈（agentic analytics stack），通过数据基础层、维护验证流程和技能（skills）分别解决概念-实体歧义、数据过时和检索失败三大错误来源。相比编码场景，数据分析的难点在于将用户问题映射到正确的数据实体，而执行 SQL 反而是简单的。Anthropic 的数据科学团队因此得以专注于因果建模、预测和机器学习等战略工作。

aihotHighlights.summary: Miso One 正式发布，一个 8B 参数的开源权重语音模型（TTS），旨在模拟真实人类朗读的温暖与节奏。它支持一次语音克隆（只需短样本），推理延迟仅 110ms。模型权重已开源至 GitHub，无需 API 即可自托管，音频数据不离开本地。API 访问即将推出。演示已上线，可先试听再克隆仓库。

items.title: Cloudflare Radar：机器人流量首次超过人类占比57.5%
items.description: Cloudflare Radar 实时统计显示，过去一周（5月28日至6月4日）全球所有 HTML 网页请求流量中，57.5% 来自机器人（爬虫、AI 抓取、自动化脚本），仅42.5%来自真人浏览器，机器人流量首次超过人类。按所有 HTTP 流量返回内容分类，JSON（API 机器通信）占33.1%居首，HTML 仅12%。互联网流量主体已从人类浏览网页转向机器间通信和机器人抓取。
items.summary: Cloudflare Radar 实时统计显示，过去一周（5月28日至6月4日）全球所有 HTML 网页请求流量中，57.5% 来自机器人（爬虫、AI 抓取、自动化脚本），仅42.5%来自真人浏览器，机器人流量首次超过人类。按所有 HTTP 流量返回内容分类，JSON（API 机器通信）占33.1%居首，HTML 仅12%。互联网流量主体已从人类浏览网页转向机器间通信和机器人抓取。

items.title: 联合国报告：2030年AI数据中心水电消耗将翻倍
items.description: 联合国大学水、环境与健康研究所报告指出，受AI需求驱动，去年全球数据中心耗电448太瓦时（AI占五分之一），耗水4.5万亿升，碳排放1.89亿吨。预计到2030年，年耗电量将翻倍至945太瓦时（AI占40%），耗水增至9.3万亿升，碳排放升至3.99亿吨，占地面积从6900平方公里扩展至14500平方公里。报告警告若忽视环境成本，AI落地还将加剧土地紧张与电子废弃物问题。

items.title: 世界模型的功能分类
items.description: World Labs团队与李飞飞发文，梳理"世界模型"这一被滥用的术语。对比语言模型学习文本统计，世界模型学习空间与时间统计（如光照、物理规律）。基于部分可观马尔可夫决策过程（POMDP）框架，智能体通过动作影响世界状态，观测是部分视图。当前被称为"世界模型"的不同系统本质上是同一循环的不同投影：第一类为渲染器，输出给人眼看的像素，以视觉保真度为核心。文章着重于概念分层，未给出具体模型名、参数或基准分数。

items.title: 洪水韧性新篇章：Google 开源水文建模框架
items.description: Google Research 在 GitHub 开源了其水文建模框架，这是一个基于 PyTorch 的 Python 包，采用与 Flood Hub 河段洪水预报相同的模型架构和训练数据。该框架允许研究者和预报员训练 AI 洪水预报模型、添加新模型与数据，并已与捷克水文气象研究所（CHMI）等合作测试。开放模型架构和训练管线旨在让各国气象水文部门在保留数据完全控制权的同时，整合基于 AI 的洪水预报。

items.title: 联合国报告：2030年AI数据中心水电消耗将翻倍
items.description: 联合国大学水、环境与健康研究所报告指出，受AI需求驱动，去年全球数据中心耗电448太瓦时（AI占五分之一），耗水4.5万亿升，碳排放1.89亿吨。预计到2030年，年耗电量将翻倍至945太瓦时（AI占40%），耗水增至9.3万亿升，碳排放升至3.99亿吨，占地面积从6900平方公里扩展至14500平方公里。报告警告若忽视环境成本，AI落地还将加剧土地紧张与电子废弃物问题。

items.title: 世界模型的功能分类
items.description: World Labs团队与李飞飞发文，梳理"世界模型"这一被滥用的术语。对比语言模型学习文本统计，世界模型学习空间与时间统计（如光照、物理规律）。基于部分可观马尔可夫决策过程（POMDP）框架，智能体通过动作影响世界状态，观测是部分视图。当前被称为"世界模型"的不同系统本质上是同一循环的不同投影：第一类为渲染器，输出给人眼看的像素，以视觉保真度为核心。文章着重于概念分层，未给出具体模型名、参数或基准分数。

items.title: 洪水韧性新篇章：Google 开源水文建模框架
items.description: Google Research 在 GitHub 开源了其水文建模框架，这是一个基于 PyTorch 的 Python 包，采用与 Flood Hub 河段洪水预报相同的模型架构和训练数据。该框架允许研究者和预报员训练 AI 洪水预报模型、添加新模型与数据，并已与捷克水文气象研究所（CHMI）等合作测试。开放模型架构和训练管线旨在让各国气象水文部门在保留数据完全控制权的同时，整合基于 AI 的洪水预报。

trendEntities.title: Cloudflare Radar：机器人流量首次超过人类占比57.5%
normalizedKeys: title:cloudflare radar 机器人流量首次超过人类占比57 5

items.title: Cloudflare Radar：机器人流量首次超过人类占比57.5%
items.summary: Cloudflare Radar 实时统计显示，过去一周（5月28日至6月4日）全球所有 HTML 网页请求流量中，57.5% 来自机器人（爬虫、AI 抓取、自动化脚本），仅42.5%来自真人浏览器，机器人流量首次超过人类。按所有 HTTP 流量返回内容分类，JSON（API 机器通信）占33.1%居首，HTML 仅12%。互联网流量主体已从人类浏览网页转向机器间通信和机器人抓取。

trendEntities.title: 联合国报告：2030年AI数据中心水电消耗将翻倍
normalizedKeys: title:联合国报告 2030年ai数据中心水电消耗将翻倍

items.title: 联合国报告：2030年AI数据中心水电消耗将翻倍
items.summary: 联合国大学水、环境与健康研究所报告指出，受AI需求驱动，去年全球数据中心耗电448太瓦时（AI占五分之一），耗水4.5万亿升，碳排放1.89亿吨。预计到2030年，年耗电量将翻倍至945太瓦时（AI占40%），耗水增至9.3万亿升，碳排放升至3.99亿吨，占地面积从6900平方公里扩展至14500平方公里。报告警告若忽视环境成本，AI落地还将加剧土地紧张与电子废弃物问题。

trendEntities.title: 世界模型的功能分类

items.title: 世界模型的功能分类
items.summary: World Labs团队与李飞飞发文，梳理"世界模型"这一被滥用的术语。对比语言模型学习文本统计，世界模型学习空间与时间统计（如光照、物理规律）。基于部分可观马尔可夫决策过程（POMDP）框架，智能体通过动作影响世界状态，观测是部分视图。当前被称为"世界模型"的不同系统本质上是同一循环的不同投影：第一类为渲染器，输出给人眼看的像素，以视觉保真度为核心。文章着重于概念分层，未给出具体模型名、参数或基准分数。

trendEntities.title: 洪水韧性新篇章：Google 开源水文建模框架
normalizedKeys: title:洪水韧性新篇章 google 开源水文建模框架

items.title: 洪水韧性新篇章：Google 开源水文建模框架
items.summary: Google Research 在 GitHub 开源了其水文建模框架，这是一个基于 PyTorch 的 Python 包，采用与 Flood Hub 河段洪水预报相同的模型架构和训练数据。该框架允许研究者和预报员训练 AI 洪水预报模型、添加新模型与数据，并已与捷克水文气象研究所（CHMI）等合作测试。开放模型架构和训练管线旨在让各国气象水文部门在保留数据完全控制权的同时，整合基于 AI 的洪水预报。

trendEntities.title: Anthropic 用 Claude 赋能自助数据分析
normalizedKeys: title:anthropic 用 claude 赋能自助数据分析

items.title: Anthropic 用 Claude 赋能自助数据分析
items.summary: Anthropic 使用 Claude 自动化了 95% 的业务分析查询，整体准确率约 95%。其关键在于构建智能体分析栈（agentic analytics stack），通过数据基础层、维护验证流程和技能（skills）分别解决概念-实体歧义、数据过时和检索失败三大错误来源。相比编码场景，数据分析的难点在于将用户问题映射到正确的数据实体，而执行 SQL 反而是简单的。Anthropic 的数据科学团队因此得以专注于因果建模、预测和机器学习等战略工作。

trendEntities.title: Miso One 开源语音模型：8B 参数、110ms 延迟、一次语音克隆
normalizedKeys: title:miso one 开源语音模型 8b 参数 110ms 延迟 一次语音克隆

items.title: Miso One 开源语音模型：8B 参数、110ms 延迟、一次语音克隆
items.summary: Miso One 正式发布，一个 8B 参数的开源权重语音模型（TTS），旨在模拟真实人类朗读的温暖与节奏。它支持一次语音克隆（只需短样本），推理延迟仅 110ms。模型权重已开源至 GitHub，无需 API 即可自托管，音频数据不离开本地。API 访问即将推出。演示已上线，可先试听再克隆仓库。

items.title: OpenShell v0.0.55 发布：新增 Vertex AI 推理支持
items.summary: OpenShell v0.0.55 🧩 Google Vertex AI 推理提供者 🔒 基于配置文件的策略可见性 🐳 网关中更好的 Podman 检测 🖥️ 恢复 GPU procfs 基准行为 🔧 CI 与文档修复 运行智能体对接 Vertex AI，同时拥有改进的策略可见性以及更可靠的 Podman 和 GPU 沙箱行为。 https：//github.com/NVIDIA/OpenShell/releases/tag/v0.0.55

trendEntities.title: NVIDIA Research 在 CVPR 2026 发表三篇论文：规模化训练实现抓取、自动驾驶与智能体泛化
normalizedKeys: title:nvidia research 在 cvpr 2026 发表三篇论文 规模化训练实现抓取 自动驾驶与智能体泛化

items.title: NVIDIA Research 在 CVPR 2026 发表三篇论文：规模化训练实现抓取、自动驾驶与智能体泛化
items.summary: NVIDIA Research 在 CVPR 2026 上发表三篇论文，展示规模化训练带来的泛化能力。GraspGen-X 是首个零样本抓取基础模型，基于 20 亿次模拟抓取训练，可为任意末端执行器生成抓取姿态。LCDrive 用紧凑潜在表示替代文本推理，让自动驾驶在嵌入式硬件上更快推理。NitroGen 基于 Isaac GR00T 架构，在大量虚拟环境中训练具身智能体。此外还发布了新的物理 AI 智能体技能，以及配合 GraspGen-X 使用的 CUDA 加速运动规划库 curoboV2 和闭环抓取论文 Grasp-MPC。

trendEntities.title: Suno完成4亿美元D轮融资

items.title: Suno完成4亿美元D轮融资
items.summary: 我们激动地宣布Suno的新篇章：4亿美元D轮融资，估值54亿美元！🚀 我们的使命一直很简单：让更多人能体验制作音乐的乐趣。非常感谢我们不可思议的社区和投资者与我们共同建设。 点击此处阅读Mikey的博客：https：//suno.com/blog/series-d-announcement

trendEntities.title: 微软与OpenAI分道扬镳--如今双方准备正面交锋
normalizedKeys: title:微软与openai分道扬镳 如今双方准备正面交锋

items.title: 微软与OpenAI分道扬镳--如今双方准备正面交锋
items.summary: 微软与OpenAI的合作关系已彻底破裂，双方进入正面竞争态势。前DeepMind高管、现任微软AI主管Mustafa Suleyman明确表示，微软现在必须从头证明自己能独立完成所有必需的任务。这标志着两家科技巨头从紧密合作伙伴转变为直接竞争对手。

trendEntities.title: 介绍 Claude Partner Network 的 Services Track 和 Partner Hub
normalizedKeys: title:介绍 claude partner network 的 services track 和 partner hub

items.title: 介绍 Claude Partner Network 的 Services Track 和 Partner Hub
items.summary: Anthropic 扩展 Claude Partner Network，推出 Services Track 分级体系和 Partner Hub 门户。Services Track 设 Select、Preferred、Global Premier 三级，按认证人数、投产客户数及客户推荐信量化评定。Partner Hub 提供每日更新仪表盘和公开目录，方便合作伙伴查看进展、客户寻找供应商。该网络三月启动以来已有超 4 万家公司申请，逾 1 万顾问获认证；Accenture 训练 3 万人，Cognizant 部署约 35 万员工，Deloitte 惠及 47 万人，KPMG 覆盖 27.6 万，Infosys 构建行业智能体，PwC 先在美推广再扩至全球。
items.description: Anthropic 扩展 Claude Partner Network，推出 Services Track 分级体系和 Partner Hub 门户。Services Track 设 Select、Preferred、Global Premier 三级，按认证人数、投产客户数及客户推荐信量化评定。Partner Hub 提供每日更新仪表盘和公开目录，方便合作伙伴查看进展、客户寻找供应商。该网络三月启动以来已有超 4 万家公司申请，逾 1 万顾问获认证；Accenture 训练 3 万人，Cognizant 部署约 35 万员工，Deloitte 惠及 47 万人，KPMG 覆盖 27.6 万，Infosys 构建行业智能体，PwC 先在美推广再扩至全球。

trendEntities.title: 欧盟公布全面技术主权计划，推动芯片与AI自主发展
normalizedKeys: title:欧盟公布全面技术主权计划 推动芯片与ai自主发展

items.title: 欧盟公布全面技术主权计划，推动芯片与AI自主发展
items.summary: 欧盟公布一项全面技术主权计划，旨在扩大本土半导体、人工智能和云计算供应链，以减少对美国和亚洲的依赖。该计划覆盖芯片制造、AI基础设施及云计算服务等多个关键领域，目标是增强欧洲在核心技术上的自主能力。

items.summary: Cursor Enterprise 正式推出 Organizations 结构，允许企业在统一面板中管理多个团队。每个团队可独立设置预算、安全策略、模型访问和功能控制。新增 Groups 作为跨团队或团队内的轻量级用户集合，用于分段管理模型访问、花销上限和智能体权限，不同设置取最宽松权限生效。管理员可创建沙箱团队预先测试新功能，再向全公司推送；也可按部门划分模型访问和预算。组织级仪表盘汇总所有团队的 token 用量与花费，支持按团队、用户等维度筛选。身份提供商和 SCIM 目录在组织层面一次配置，成员自动同步。该功能现已全面开放给所有 Enterprise 客户。

trendEntities.title: Anthropic 分析 832 个 AI 恶意账户：中高风险攻击者半年从 33% 跃至 56%
normalizedKeys: title:anthropic 分析 832 个 ai 恶意账户 中高风险攻击者半年从 33 跃至 56

items.title: Anthropic 分析 832 个 AI 恶意账户：中高风险攻击者半年从 33% 跃至 56%
items.summary: Anthropic 分析 2025 年 3 月至 2026 年 3 月间 832 个被封禁的恶意账户，映射至 MITRE ATT&CK 框架。67.3% 使用 AI 编写恶意软件，6.5% 用于横向移动。六个月间中高风险攻击者占比从 33% 升至 56%。AI 用于账户发现增长 8.9%，AI 辅助钓鱼下降 8.6%。传统基于技术数量或平台（Claude Code、API、聊天界面）的威胁评估失效，而 MITRE ATT&CK 框架尚未收录此类智能体编排行为。

items.summary: DeepSeek 计划在首轮融资中从腾讯、宁德时代等投资方处筹集约 500 亿元人民币。融资落地后，其投后估值预计在 3500 亿至 4000 亿元。创始人梁文峰将出资 200 亿元，腾讯和宁德时代分别拟投资 100 亿元和 50 亿元，成为最大外部投资者。DeepSeek 去年凭借 V3 大模型与 R1 推理模型获得广泛认可。此次融资凸显了中国正打造从大模型到算力能源基建的全链条 AI 产业。

items.title: STRIDE: Training Data Attribution via Sparse Recovery from Subset Perturbations

items.summary: Training Data Attribution (TDA) seeks to trace a model's predictions back to its training data. The gold standard for TDA relies on causal interventions, observing how a model changes when data is added or removed, but repeated retraining is computationally challenging for Large Language Models (LLMs). Consequently, most approaches approximate this effect in the parameter space using gradients. However, tracking gradients across billions of parameters is not only prohibitively expensive but relies on local approximations. In this work, we propose a shift: rather than estimating parameter changes, we model the functional effect of training data in the activation space. We introduce STRIDE (Steering-based Training Data Influence Decomposition), a framework that formulates TDA as a sparse recovery problem in the spirit of compressive sensing. STRIDE learns lightweight "steering operators" that mimic the behavioral shift caused by training on data subsets. By measuring how these operators perturb test predictions, we recover individual training example influences via sparse linear decomposition. STRIDE achieves state-of-the-art for LLM pre-training attribution while being an order of magnitude ($13\times$) faster than previous art. We further validate its practical utility through downstream applications including data selection, data contamination, and qualitative analysis.
items.author: Rishit Dagli, Abir Harrasse, Luke Zhang, Florent Draye, Amirali Abdullah, Bernhard Schölkopf, Zhijing Jin
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: large language model, llm

items.title: Streaming Communication in Multi-Agent Reasoning
items.description: Multi-agent reasoning systems adopt a "generate-then-transfer" paradigm that forces end-to-end latency to scale linearly with pipeline depth. We introduce StreamMA, a multi-agent reasoning system that streams each reasoning step to downstream agents as soon as it is generated, pipelining adjacent agents and thus reducing latency. items.summary: Multi-agent reasoning systems adopt a "generate-then-transfer" paradigm that forces end-to-end latency to scale linearly with pipeline depth. We introduce StreamMA, a multi-agent reasoning system that streams each reasoning step to downstream agents as soon as it is generated, pipelining adjacent agents and thus reducing latency. Surprisingly, this pipelining also improves effectiveness: because multi-step reasoning quality is non-uniform and early steps are more reliable than later ones, working with these reliable early steps instead of the full chain prevents error-prone late steps from misleading downstream agents. We formalize both advantages with the first closed-form joint analysis of stream, serial, and single protocols, deriving the effectiveness ordering, speedup upper bound, and cost ratio. Across eight reasoning benchmarks spanning mathematics, science, and code, two frontier LLMs (Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5.4), and three topologies (Chain, Tree, Graph), StreamMA outperforms both baselines (avg. +7.3 pp, max +22.4 pp on HMMT 2026; Claude Opus 4.6-high). Beyond these contributions, we discover a "step-level scaling law": increasing per-agent steps consistently improves both effectiveness and efficiency, a new scaling dimension orthogonal to and composable with agent-count scaling.
items.author: Zhen Yang, Xiaogang Xu, Wen Wang, Cong Chen, Xander Xu, Ying-Cong Chen
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: llm, agent, multi-agent, benchmark

items.title: Reinforcement Learning from Rich Feedback with Distributional DAgger
items.description: Reasoning models have advanced rapidly, but the dominant reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) recipe remains surprisingly narrow: sample many responses and reward each with a single bit indicating whether the final answer is correct. Yet many settings provide rich feedback, including execution traces, tool outputs, expert corrections, and model self-evaluations. items.summary: Reasoning models have advanced rapidly, but the dominant reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) recipe remains surprisingly narrow: sample many responses and reward each with a single bit indicating whether the final answer is correct. Yet many settings provide rich feedback, including execution traces, tool outputs, expert corrections, and model self-evaluations. We study how to use such feedback through a distributional variant of the classic imitation learning algorithm DAgger, where the learner has local access to an expert distribution on states visited by the current policy. This yields a simple forward cross-entropy objective that admits a blackbox expert and whose sequence-level gradient {conduct rich credit assignment by propagating} future expert-student disagreement back to earlier decisions. We show that prior RL with self-distillation objectives based on reverse KL or Jensen-Shannon fail to guarantee monotonic policy improvement: even when the expert has higher reward, their updates may increase probability on worse actions. In contrast, we show that forward cross-entropy admits monotonic policy improvement and enjoys guarantees on regret. We further show that our objective optimizes a lower bound on teacher-weighted likelihood of success, leading to improved Pass@N. Empirically, our approach, DistIL, improves over RLVR and RL with self-distillation baselines across a variety of domains: scientific reasoning, coding, and solving hard mathematical problems.
items.author: Rishabh Agrawal, Jacob Fein-Ashley, Paria Rashidinejad
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: evaluation

trendEntities.title: Multi-Column RBF Neural Network Using Adaptive and Non-Adaptive Particle Swarm Optimization
normalizedKeys: title:multi column rbf neural network using adaptive and non adaptive particle swarm optimization

items.title: Multi-Column RBF Neural Network Using Adaptive and Non-Adaptive Particle Swarm Optimization
items.description: The radial basis function neural network (RBFN) trained with a gradient descending algorithm provides an effective fully connected structure in both shallow and deep networks. The error correction (ErrCor), a state-of-the-art gradient-based training method, selects optimal hidden units to improve accuracy. Alternatively, as a population-based algorithm, the particle swarm optimization algorithm (PSO) uses the swarm experience to optimize RBFN parameters, offering global search and robustness to
items.summary: The radial basis function neural network (RBFN) trained with a gradient descending algorithm provides an effective fully connected structure in both shallow and deep networks. The error correction (ErrCor), a state-of-the-art gradient-based training method, selects optimal hidden units to improve accuracy. Alternatively, as a population-based algorithm, the particle swarm optimization algorithm (PSO) uses the swarm experience to optimize RBFN parameters, offering global search and robustness to local minima. Adaptive PSO (APSO) has emerged as an improved variant of PSO. APSO algorithm improves convergence speed by dynamically adjusting swarm parameters during optimization. Both ErrCor and PSO demonstrate improved results and competitive convergence. However, with large datasets, these methods face scalability challenges such as excessive kernel computations and large hidden layer structures. A recent multi-column RBFN approach (MCRN) improves ErrCor performance by deploying small RBFNs in a parallel system. Inspired by MCRN's success, we propose two novel approaches to improve PSO performance: the multi-column RBFN with PSO (MC-PSO) and the multi-column RBFN with APSO (MC-APSO). These methods introduce parallel RBFN structures trained using evolutionary swarm methods. Each RBFN is independently trained on a specific spatial subset of the dataset using either PSO or APSO algorithms. These resulting specialist-trained RBFNs are tailored to their respective subsets. During testing, only selected RBFNs, where the test instance neighbors are located, contribute to the multi-column output. This specialization improves accuracy, while parallelism enhances speed. We evaluate the proposed methods on various benchmark datasets. The MC-PSO and MC-APSO outperform ErrCor, PSO, APSO, and MCRN in terms of accuracy and recall. They also demonstrate faster training and testing times in most experiments.
items.author: Ammar Hoori, Yuichi Motai
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: benchmark

Adaptive PSO (APSO) has emerged as an improved variant of PSO. APSO algorithm improves convergence speed by dynamically adjusting swarm parameters during optimization. Both ErrCor and PSO demonstrate improved results and competitive convergence. However, with large datasets, these methods face scalability challenges such as excessive kernel computations and large hidden layer structures. A recent multi-column RBFN approach (MCRN) improves ErrCor performance by deploying small RBFNs in a parallel system. Inspired by MCRN's success, we propose two novel approaches to improve PSO performance: the multi-column RBFN with PSO (MC-PSO) and the multi-column RBFN with APSO (MC-APSO). These methods introduce parallel RBFN structures trained using evolutionary swarm methods. Each RBFN is independently trained on a specific spatial subset of the dataset using either PSO or APSO algorithms. These resulting specialist-trained RBFNs are tailored to their respective subsets. During testing, only selected RBFNs, where the test instance neighbors are located, contribute to the multi-column output. This specialization improves accuracy, while parallelism enhances speed. We evaluate the proposed methods on various benchmark datasets. The MC-PSO and MC-APSO outperform ErrCor, PSO, APSO, and MCRN in terms of accuracy and recall. They also demonstrate faster training and testing times in most experiments.

trendEntities.title: An Open-Source Two-Stage Computer Vision Pipeline for Fine-Grained Vehicle Classification using Vision Transformers
normalizedKeys: title:an open source two stage computer vision pipeline for fine grained vehicle classification using vision transformers

items.title: An Open-Source Two-Stage Computer Vision Pipeline for Fine-Grained Vehicle Classification using Vision Transformers
items.description: Vehicle body type is a significant determinant of cyclist injury severity in overtaking crashes, yet automated tools for classifying vehicles into injury-risk-relevant categories from naturalistic roadway video do not exist in the open literature. Standard object detection benchmarks provide only coarse vehicle labels (car, truck, bus, motorcycle), while existing fine-grained recognition systems are trained on controlled imagery and lack evaluation for deployment robustness across recording site
items.summary: Vehicle body type is a significant determinant of cyclist injury severity in overtaking crashes, yet automated tools for classifying vehicles into injury-risk-relevant categories from naturalistic roadway video do not exist in the open literature. Standard object detection benchmarks provide only coarse vehicle labels (car, truck, bus, motorcycle), while existing fine-grained recognition systems are trained on controlled imagery and lack evaluation for deployment robustness across recording sites. This paper presents an open-source two-stage computer vision pipeline combining a pre-trained RT-DETR detector for coarse vehicle localization with a fine-tuned Vision Transformer (ViT-Base/16) for six-category body-type classification: passenger car, SUV, pickup truck, minivan, large van, and commercial truck. A confidence-based abstention mechanism withholds Stage 2 predictions when softmax output falls below 0.60, producing unknown labels rather than silent misclassifications. Evaluated on 3,805 annotated overtaking events from a bicycle-lane corridor in Ann Arbor, Michigan (in-distribution), the pipeline achieved 0.94 accuracy with per-class F1 scores from 0.91 (minivan) to 0.97 (SUV). On an independent out-of-distribution evaluation of 311 events from an open cycling dataset without retraining, accuracy was 0.89. Three of four well-represented categories maintained F1 at or above 0.90 under domain shift. The largest degradation was observed for minivan (F1 = 0.72), driven by abstention rate rising from 2.4% to 25.0% rather than active misclassification, consistent with the mechanism propagating genuine model uncertainty. The full pipeline, including inference scripts, training code, evaluation utilities, and model weights, is released as open-source software to support reproducibility and reuse across roadside video archives and cycling safety research.
items.author: Gandhimathi Padmanaban, Fred Feng
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: safety, benchmark, evaluation

trendEntities.title: GeM-NR: Geometry-Aware Multi-View Editing for Nonrigid Scene Changes
normalizedKeys: title:gem nr geometry aware multi view editing for nonrigid scene changes

items.title: GeM-NR: Geometry-Aware Multi-View Editing for Nonrigid Scene Changes
items.description: Recent developments in multi-view image editing with generative models have brought us a step closer toward general 3D content generation and customization. Most existing works focus on rigid or appearance-only edits by utilizing the geometry of the unedited scene. This naturally limits these methods to edits that preserve the underlying scene structure. Other approaches are trained for specific image editing tasks, such as object removal and addition. Despite this progress, general nonrigid edi
items.summary: Recent developments in multi-view image editing with generative models have brought us a step closer toward general 3D content generation and customization. Most existing works focus on rigid or appearance-only edits by utilizing the geometry of the unedited scene. This naturally limits these methods to edits that preserve the underlying scene structure. Other approaches are trained for specific image editing tasks, such as object removal and addition. Despite this progress, general nonrigid edits, i.e., edits that substantially change the scene geometry, remain challenging for existing methods. We propose GeM-NR, a fast and flexible training-free approach for general multi-view consistent image editing, including edits that drastically change the geometry and appearance of the scene. Given an anchor image edited with a chosen backbone editor (such as FLUX, Qwen, BrushNet) and a query unedited image, GeM-NR edits the query image consistently with the anchor edit. The method incorporates multiple stages: (i) depth map estimation, where we propose a strategy to maximize the alignment between the 3D point clouds of the edited and unedited scenes, (ii) projection onto a query viewpoint, and (iii) refinement of the obtained image conditioned on the unedited query. The conditioning-based formulation scales well from two to many views of an object. We demonstrate the ability of our method to handle edits with significant changes in geometry and appearance, something that existing methods struggle with. We perform an extensive evaluation showing that our method improves consistency for a wide variety of edit tasks, including generating 3D representations of the edited scene. Both quantitative and qualitative results indicate the state-of-the-art performance of our method in terms of edit quality as well as geometric and photometric consistency across multiple views.
items.author: Josef Bengtson, Yaroslava Lochman, Fredrik Kahl
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: alignment, evaluation

trendEntities.title: BBOmix: A Tabular Benchmark for Hyperparameter Optimization of Unsupervised Biological Representation Learning
normalizedKeys: title:bbomix a tabular benchmark for hyperparameter optimization of unsupervised biological representation learning

items.title: BBOmix: A Tabular Benchmark for Hyperparameter Optimization of Unsupervised Biological Representation Learning
items.description: The rapid advancement of high-throughput sequencing has led to large, high-dimensional omics datasets. Deep unsupervised learning architectures, particularly Autoencoders (AEs), are increasingly used for dimensionality reduction and representation learning in this domain. However, AEs are highly sensitive to architectural choices and hyperparameters, and unsupervised optimization typically relies on reconstruction loss, which may be a poor proxy for downstream utility. Exhaustive hyperparameter
items.summary: The rapid advancement of high-throughput sequencing has led to large, high-dimensional omics datasets. Deep unsupervised learning architectures, particularly Autoencoders (AEs), are increasingly used for dimensionality reduction and representation learning in this domain. However, AEs are highly sensitive to architectural choices and hyperparameters, and unsupervised optimization typically relies on reconstruction loss, which may be a poor proxy for downstream utility. Exhaustive hyperparameter optimization (HPO) is computationally expensive, leading researchers to frequently rely on suboptimal default configurations. To democratize access to large-scale unsupervised HPO research, we introduce $\textbf{BBOmix}$, the first open-source tabular benchmark for unsupervised representation learning on real-world biological data. Our benchmark includes 105,000 evaluations across four AE architectures and seven multi-omics modalities from the TCGA and SCHC datasets. We quantify the correlation between reconstruction loss and downstream task performance and provide an extensive evaluation of state-of-the-art single-fidelity, multi-fidelity, and transfer learning HPO methods, establishing a rigorous baseline for future research in unsupervised biological representation learning.
items.author: Luca Thale-Bombien, Jan Ewald, Ralf König, Aaron Klein
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: benchmark, evaluation

trendEntities.title: Activation-Based Active Learning for In-Context Learning: Challenges and Insights
normalizedKeys: title:activation based active learning for in context learning challenges and insights

items.title: Activation-Based Active Learning for In-Context Learning: Challenges and Insights
items.description: Deep active learning has previously been explored for LLM in-context sample selection, but not with methods that utilise recent advances in understanding of transformer activations. In this paper, we test the hypothesis that model activations could provide a fine-grained signal to optimise the selection of in-context examples. We present the most comprehensive analysis to date of MLP activation-based deep active learning methods applied to in-context learning, including how different attention m
items.summary: Deep active learning has previously been explored for LLM in-context sample selection, but not with methods that utilise recent advances in understanding of transformer activations. In this paper, we test the hypothesis that model activations could provide a fine-grained signal to optimise the selection of in-context examples. We present the most comprehensive analysis to date of MLP activation-based deep active learning methods applied to in-context learning, including how different attention masking strategies impact active learning across diverse classification and generative datasets, using both Llama-3.2-3B and Qwen2.5-3B base models. However, we find a negative result: MLP outputs, viewed through the lenses of massive activations or the first four moments, do not correlate with example quality or task performance. Specifically, the absolute Spearman correlation coefficient is at most 0.33 for all tasks and models we tested, showing that such activation-based sampling should not be used for in-context learning. We hypothesise that this may be due to superposition, whereby models represent more features than they have dimensionality, suggesting that methods like Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) may be a promising future direction.
items.author: Yaseen M. Osman, Geoff V. Merrett, Stuart E. Middleton
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: llm

trendEntities.title: Towards Efficient and Evidence-grounded Mobility Prediction with LLM-Driven Agent
normalizedKeys: title:towards efficient and evidence grounded mobility prediction with llm driven agent

items.title: Towards Efficient and Evidence-grounded Mobility Prediction with LLM-Driven Agent
items.description: Individual-level mobility prediction is central to urban simulation, transportation planning, and policy analysis. Supervised sequence models achieve strong accuracy but require task-specific training and offer limited decision-level transparency. Recent LLM-based methods improve interpretability, yet mostly rely on static prompts and single-pass inference, limiting their ability to seek additional evidence when mobility signals are weak or conflicting. We propose \method{}, a training-free LLM-
items.summary: Individual-level mobility prediction is central to urban simulation, transportation planning, and policy analysis. Supervised sequence models achieve strong accuracy but require task-specific training and offer limited decision-level transparency. Recent LLM-based methods improve interpretability, yet mostly rely on static prompts and single-pass inference, limiting their ability to seek additional evidence when mobility signals are weak or conflicting. We propose \method{}, a training-free LLM-driven agent framework that formulates next-location prediction as adaptive evidence-controlled decision making. \method{} resolves routine cases through a fast path based on historical regularity, while ambiguous cases trigger iterative tool use over recent trajectories, historical behavior, stay-move likelihood, and geographical evidence. Across three mobility datasets, AgentMob achieves the strongest overall performance among training-free LLM-based methods, with GPT-5.4 reaching 71.42\% Acc@1 on BW, 33.14\% on YJMob100K, and 33.50\% on Shanghai ISP. On BW non-fast-path cases, the LLM controller improves Acc@1 from 30.65\% to 48.62\% over a same-tool statistical baseline, showing that its main benefit lies in resolving ambiguous predictions through adaptive evidence gathering. Our code is available at https://github.com/Unknown-zoo/AgentMob.
items.author: Linyao Chen, Qinlao Zhao, Zechen Li, Mingming Li, Likun Ni, Jinyu Chen, Yuhao Yao, Xuan Song, Noboru Koshizuka, Hiroki Kobayashi
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: llm, agent, tool use

trendEntities.title: Self-Evaluation Is Already There: Eliciting Latent Judge Calibration in Base LLMs with Minimal Data
normalizedKeys: title:self evaluation is already there eliciting latent judge calibration in base llms with minimal data

items.title: Self-Evaluation Is Already There: Eliciting Latent Judge Calibration in Base LLMs with Minimal Data
items.description: Large language models are increasingly evaluated by other models, raising a natural question: can a model predict how a judge will score its own output? We find that the ability is largely present before any targeted training: prompted few-shot, a base model already predicts an external judge's multi-attribute quality scores on open-ended responses well above chance across three benchmarks. We introduce Self-Evaluation Elicitation (SEE), a method that surfaces this latent ability through a short
items.summary: Large language models are increasingly evaluated by other models, raising a natural question: can a model predict how a judge will score its own output? We find that the ability is largely present before any targeted training: prompted few-shot, a base model already predicts an external judge's multi-attribute quality scores on open-ended responses well above chance across three benchmarks. We introduce Self-Evaluation Elicitation (SEE), a method that surfaces this latent ability through a short cycle comprising a calibration-coupled reinforcement learning phase that improves the answer and predicts the judge, followed by a masked distillation phase that sharpens the prediction while leaving the answer untouched. From 160 unique examples, roughly 31x fewer than a reinforcement learning baseline, SEE improves held-out calibration across three benchmarks while preserving answer quality. The elicited self-evaluation is sharply localized within the model's own token distribution and stable across judges it was never trained against, indicating a transferable notion of quality rather than a single judge's preference. These results reframe judge-aligned self-evaluation as a problem of elicitation rather than acquisition.
items.author: XiuYu Zhang, Yi Shan, Junfeng Fang, Zhenkai Liang
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: large language model, llm, benchmark, evaluation

sourceItems.author: XiuYu Zhang, Yi Shan, Junfeng Fang, Zhenkai Liang
sourceItems.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: large language model, llm, benchmark, evaluation

trendEntities.summary: Large language models are increasingly evaluated by other models, raising a natural question: can a model predict how a judge will score its own output? We find that the ability is largely present before any targeted training: prompted few-shot, a base model already predicts an external judge's multi-attribute quality scores on open-ended responses well above chance across three benchmarks. We introduce Self-Evaluation Elicitation (SEE), a method that surfaces this latent ability through a short cycle comprising a calibration-coupled reinforcement learning phase that improves the answer and predicts the judge, followed by a masked distillation phase that sharpens the prediction while leaving the answer untouched. From 160 unique examples, roughly 31x fewer than a reinforcement learning baseline, SEE improves held-out calibration across three benchmarks while preserving answer quality. The elicited self-evaluation is sharply localized within the model's own token distribution and stable across judges it was never trained against, indicating a transferable notion of quality rather than a single judge's preference. These results reframe judge-aligned self-evaluation as a problem of elicitation rather than acquisition.

trendEntities.title: Audio Interaction Model

items.title: Audio Interaction Model
items.description: Audio is an inherently interactive modality, yet today's Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) are offline, and streaming audio models each handle only a single task such as streaming ASR or voice chatting. It is time to unify them into one online LALM: a model that, through an always-on perceive-decide-respond loop, listens to sound, environment, and instructions in real time and reacts on the fly. We formalize this regime as the Audio Interaction Model, and realize it with Audio-Interaction, a u
items.summary: Audio is an inherently interactive modality, yet today's Large Audio Language Models (LALMs) are offline, and streaming audio models each handle only a single task such as streaming ASR or voice chatting. It is time to unify them into one online LALM: a model that, through an always-on perceive-decide-respond loop, listens to sound, environment, and instructions in real time and reacts on the fly. We formalize this regime as the Audio Interaction Model, and realize it with Audio-Interaction, a unified streaming model that retains offline task execution while adding online general audio instruction following, from dialogue to full voice chatting, deciding when to respond from the semantics of the stream. To enable this, we propose SoundFlow, a framework that instantiates the perceive-decide-respond loop end to end, from data to training to deployment, through streaming-native data construction, comprehension-aware training, and asynchronous low-latency inference for stable real-time interaction. We further construct StreamAudio-2M, a 2.6M-item streaming corpus spanning 7 fundamental abilities and 28 sub-tasks, and Proactive-Sound-Bench for evaluating proactive audio intervention. Across 8 benchmarks, Audio-Interaction preserves competitive performance on mainstream audio tasks while unlocking capabilities inaccessible to offline LALMs, including real-time ASR, streaming audio instruction following, and proactive help.
items.author: Zhifei Xie, Zihang Liu, Ze An, Xiaobin Hu, Yue Liao, Ziyang Ma, Dongchao Yang, Mingbao Lin, Deheng Ye, Shuicheng Yan, Chunyan Miao

trendEntities.title: Graph Set Transformer

items.title: Graph Set Transformer
items.description: We introduce the Graph Set Transformer (GST), a neural network architecture for learning on sets of graphs, designed for tasks in which per-element predictions depend on set-wide context as well as local structure. Existing architectures, including DeepSets and SetTransformer, require pre-encoded graph embeddings from a separate GNN, creating a bottleneck between feature extraction and set-level contextualisation. In contrast, GST interleaves node-level feature propagation and cross-graph contex
items.summary: We introduce the Graph Set Transformer (GST), a neural network architecture for learning on sets of graphs, designed for tasks in which per-element predictions depend on set-wide context as well as local structure. Existing architectures, including DeepSets and SetTransformer, require pre-encoded graph embeddings from a separate GNN, creating a bottleneck between feature extraction and set-level contextualisation. In contrast, GST interleaves node-level feature propagation and cross-graph contextual modelling at every layer, fusing the two levels of information through a gating mechanism. We evaluate GST on a controlled synthetic suite designed to isolate set-conditional structural reasoning and on three real-data benchmarks spanning per-atom reaction-centre identification, reaction yield prediction, and image classification. Under matched parameter budgets, GST performs better than the baselines across these settings. An architectural ablation strongly suggests that the interleaving of local and set context contributes substantially to this advantage.
items.author: Jose E. Escrig Molina, Baoquan Chen, Daniel Probst
items.recommendedReason: Matched AI developer keywords: embedding, benchmark

items.title: Continual Visual and Verbal Learning Through a Child's Egocentric Input
items.description: Children learn the meanings of words from a continuous, temporally structured stream of egocentric experience. Recent work shows that neural networks can also learn word-referent mappings from a child's egocentric video recordings, but they cycle through the shuffled data for hundreds of epochs, contrasting with how children actually encounter their environment. We introduce BabyCL, a continual multimodal learning framework that processes the SAYCam dataset in a single chronological pass, combin
items.summary: Children learn the meanings of words from a continuous, temporally structured stream of egocentric experience. Recent work shows that neural networks can also learn word-referent mappings from a child's egocentric video recordings, but they cycle through the shuffled data for hundreds of epochs, contrasting with how children actually encounter their environment. We introduce BabyCL, a continual multimodal learning framework that processes the SAYCam dataset in a single chronological pass, combining streaming visual representation learning with an image-text contrastive objective. BabyCL combines a multi-stage temporal segmentation of the stream with a dual replay buffer that independently manages visual and multimodal histories, and it is jointly trained with three contrastive losses on a shared backbone. Under a matched optimization budget, BabyCL outperforms streaming learning baselines on the SAYCam Labeled-S 4AFC benchmark, substantially narrowing the gap to an upper bound of offline training. Ablations show that the gains are robust to the length of the online temporal segmentation window and the eviction rule of the replay buffer. Together, these results show that meaningful word-referent mappings can emerge under training conditions much closer to a child's actual experience.
items.author: Xiaoyang Jiang, Yanlai Yang, Kenneth A. Norman, Brenden Lake, Mengye Ren
